passages.text: But of this account, which holds forever, people forever prove uncomprehending, both before they have heard it and when once they have heard it. For, although all things happen in accordance with this account, they are like people without experience when they experience words and deeds such as I set forth, distinguishing (as I do) each thing according to (its) real constitution, ie, pointing out how it is. The rest of mankind, however, fail to be aware of what they do after they wake up just as they forget what they do while asleep.
passages.context: The opening of Heraclitus' book: the logos holds forever, yet people remain deaf to it both before and after hearing it.

passages.text: That is why one must follow that which is (common) [ie, universal. For 'common' means 'universal']. Though the account is common, the many live, however, as though they had a private understanding.
passages.context: The logos is shared and universal, yet people retreat into private worlds as if cut off from the common account.

passages.text: Things grasped together: things whole, things not whole; (something) being brought together, (something) being separated; (something) consonant, (something) dissonant. Out of all things (comes?) one thing, and out of one thing all things.
passages.context: The cosmos is constituted by the tension between opposites that are simultaneously grasped as one unified whole.

passages.text: As they step into the same rivers, different and (still) different waters flow upon them.
passages.context: The river is the same river precisely because it is always different waters — identity maintained through perpetual change.

passages.text: We step and do not step into the same rivers; we are and are not.
passages.context: A companion fragment to F12: personal identity is as fluid as the river — we simultaneously are and are not the same self.

passages.text: How would one escape the notice of that which never sets?
passages.context: The logos — or the divine fire — is that which never sets, from which nothing can hide, an ever-present witness.

passages.text: If (he) doesn't expect (the) unexpected, (he) will not discover (it); for (it) is difficult to discover and intractable.
passages.context: Truth belongs to those who actively open themselves to what cannot be predicted — wisdom requires expecting the unhoped-for.

passages.text: Those who seek gold dig up a great deal of earth and find little.
passages.context: Wisdom is precious and rare — those who seek it must be prepared to sift through vast quantities of dross to find the vein.

passages.text: (The ordered?) world, the same for all, no god or man made, but it always was, is, and will be, an everliving fire, being kindled in measures and being put out in measures.
passages.context: The cosmos is uncreated and eternal — an everliving fire whose kindling and extinction are governed by measure, not by any divine will.

passages.text: For souls it is death to become water, and for water death to become earth. Water comes into existence out of earth, and soul out of water.
passages.context: The elements undergo reciprocal deaths and births — soul descends into matter, matter rises back into soul, in a continuous cosmic cycle.

passages.text: Fire lives the death of earth and air lives the death of fire; water lives the death of air, earth that of water.
passages.context: Each element lives by consuming the death of another — the cosmos is a perpetual cycle of mutual transformation rather than simple destruction.

passages.text: A lot of learning does not teach (a person the possession of) understanding; (could it do so,) it would have so taught Hesiod and Pythagoras, or for that matter (?) Xenophanes and Hecataeus.
passages.context: Heraclitus distinguishes polymathia — the mere accumulation of knowledge — from nous, the insight that grasps the logos beneath appearances.

passages.text: [He says that] the wise (thing) is a single (thing) (or, differently punctuated: one thing, the wise thing, (is)) - knowing the plan twhich steerst all things through all things.
passages.context: True wisdom is singular: to know the one principle — the plan or logos — by which all things are steered through all things.

passages.text: One would never discover the limits of soul, should one traverse every road - so deep a measure does it possess.
passages.context: The soul's logos is bottomless — no outer journey can exhaust it; self-knowledge requires descending into an inexhaustible interior depth.

passages.text: 'Not after listening to me, but after listening to the account, one does wisely in agreeing that all things are (in fact?) one (thing),' [says Heraclitus].
passages.context: Heraclitus deflects authority from himself to the logos — wisdom lies not in following the man but in hearing the account that declares all is one.

passages.text: They do not understand how, while differing from (or: being at variance), (it) is in agreement with itself. (There is) a back-turning connection, like (that) of a bow or lyre.
passages.context: The bow and lyre hold their tension by pulling in opposite directions — reality's harmony is maintained by the very opposition that appears to divide it.

passages.text: An unapparent connection is stronger (or: better) than one which is obvious.
passages.context: The deepest bonds — in nature, in thought, in the logos — are invisible; what appears disconnected on the surface is most tightly joined beneath.

passages.text: A road up (and) down (is) one and the same (road).
passages.context: Opposites share a single identity — the path upward and the path downward are not two roads but one road traversed in two directions.

passages.text: One must realize that war is common, and justice strife, and that all things come to be through strife and are (so) tordainedt.
passages.context: Strife is not the enemy of order but its source — war is the father that differentiates gods from men, free from slave, all things from their opposites.

passages.text: War is father of all, and king of all. He renders some gods, others men; he makes some slaves, others free.
passages.context: War as cosmic principle: it is the generative force that produces all distinctions and hierarchies in the world.

passages.text: The totality of things, [says Heraclitus,] is an exchange for fire, and fire an exchange for all things, in the way goods (are an exchange) for gold, and gold for goods.
passages.context: Fire functions as the universal currency of the cosmos — all things are transformations of fire and all return to it, as gold mediates economic exchange.

passages.text: [Heraclitus, as though he has made some mighty and august utterance, says:] I investigated myself (or: I made enquiry of myself).
passages.context: Heraclitus enacts the Delphic injunction — self-investigation is the philosophical method, because the logos of the soul is where the common logos is most directly encountered.

passages.text: Sound thinking (is) a very great virtue, and (practical) wisdom (consists in our) saying what is true and acting in accordance with (the) real constitution (of things), (by) paying heed (to it).
passages.context: Wisdom is not contemplation alone but is unified with speech and action — the wise person speaks truly and acts in accord with how things really are.

passages.text: Those who (would) speak with insight must base themselves firmly on that which is common to all, as a city does upon (its) law - and much more firmly! For all human laws are nourished by one (law), the divine (law). For it holds sway to the extent that it wishes, and suffices for all, and is still left over.
passages.context: The divine logos underwrites all human law — speaking with genuine insight requires grounding in this one common principle that is inexhaustible and universal.

passages.text: (A thing's? (the world's?)) real constitution [according to Heraclitus] has a tendency to conceal itself.
passages.context: Nature — physis — actively hides its own structure; the logos is not self-evident but must be hunted out beneath appearances through the work of inquiry.
